title: Commercial Roofing in Haughton, LA | Commercial Roofers of Shreveport
metaDescription: Roof repair and installation for Haughton's growing I-20 retail strip and expanding school district facilities in Bossier Parish, Louisiana.

html:
Haughton: An I-20 Bedroom Town Building Out Fast

Haughton sits right off I-20 in Bossier Parish, and population growth here has translated directly into new school buildings and new retail construction over the last several years. It's a smaller commercial roof market than Bossier City or Benton, but the two things driving it, schools and retail, both come with their own scheduling demands.

School District Expansion

New classroom buildings, gymnasiums, and support facilities built to keep pace with enrollment growth mean a steady stream of school roofing work in and around Haughton, most of it TPO or another single-ply system chosen for long-term, low-maintenance performance on a public budget. School roof work runs almost entirely around the academic calendar: summer break is the only real window for anything beyond emergency repair, and we plan projects months ahead to fit that constraint.

Gymnasium roofs in particular carry wide clear spans with fewer penetrations than a classroom wing, but any leak over a gym floor becomes a liability concern fast, and we treat those roofs with that in mind even when the membrane itself looks fine on inspection.

New Retail Along the I-20 Corridor

Retail construction feeding off the town's growth has added new strip centers and standalone buildings near the interstate interchange, most on TPO over metal deck. As with any newly built commercial roof, the priority here is catching installation problems while a contractor's warranty still applies, not waiting until a leak shows up after the building owner has taken over responsibility.

  • Flashing errors at newly installed rooftop units on retail build-outs
  • Undersized or mismatched drainage on school additions built in phases
  • Ponding on wide-span gymnasium roofs with limited drainage points
  • Warranty-affecting punch-list items on new retail construction
  • Sealant failure at penetrations added after original school construction

Working Around a School Calendar

Every school roof project in Haughton gets scoped against the academic year first and the roof condition second, because a scope that can't be completed inside a summer break window creates real problems for a district trying to open on schedule in August. We build in weather contingency time on top of the base schedule, since a single delayed week from rain can compress the whole project against the start of the school year.

Weather and a Growing Roof Population

Spring hail and severe thunderstorms are the biggest risk to Haughton's newer flat roofs, both the school additions and the retail construction near the interstate, since wide, exposed low-slope surfaces can take damage that isn't obvious until a leak develops. We inspect proactively after any significant storm rather than waiting for a report.

Documentation That Holds Up for a Public Budget

School district roof work runs through a public procurement process that private retail construction doesn't, and that means every repair recommendation needs to be documented in a way a school board or a facilities director can defend to a budget committee. We write scopes that separate immediate safety repairs from planned capital work, with photos and condition notes that stand up when the district has to justify the spend.

Retail property owners along the I-20 corridor need a different kind of documentation, mostly focused on protecting a manufacturer's warranty and giving a lender or an insurer a clear record of roof condition at key points in the building's life. We tailor the paperwork to whichever audience actually needs to sign off on the work.

Access and Staging on a Working Campus

A school campus during the school year is never truly empty, even during a planned repair window, and roof access has to account for bus loops, pickup and drop-off traffic, and after-school activities that don't stop just because a crew is on-site. We schedule material staging and noisy work around those patterns rather than assuming a school parking lot is available whenever we need it.

The same access planning applies to retail construction sites still under active build-out near the interstate, where a roofing crew often has to coordinate with several other trades working the same building at once. We confirm the site schedule before showing up rather than assuming our crew has the roof to itself.

Questions Haughton Facilities Staff Ask Us

Can school roof work really only happen in summer?

Most of it, yes. Anything beyond an emergency repair gets planned around the summer break window, and we build weather contingency into the schedule so a rainy week doesn't push the project past the start of school.

Why do gymnasium roofs need special attention if the membrane looks fine?

Wide clear spans mean fewer support points and fewer drains relative to the roof area, so ponding and drainage issues can develop differently than on a standard classroom wing, even when the surface looks intact.

Should new retail construction near the interstate get inspected before opening?

Yes. Catching installation issues while the contractor's warranty is active saves the building owner from paying for a fix later that should have been covered.

How fast is growth actually changing the roofing needs out here?

Noticeably. New school additions and new retail construction have added more commercial low-slope roof area in Haughton over the past several years than the town saw in the couple of decades before that.

What happens if a project runs into a rain delay during summer break?

We build contingency days into the schedule from the start and prioritize dry-in over cosmetic finishing if the calendar gets tight, so the district isn't left with an exposed roof when students return, and we document the delay for the district's own project records.
